obligations.reason: The debug slave interface (dm_slave) provides a path to on-chip peripherals that may bypass privilege checks (priv_lvl_i) and peripheral lock bits (reglk_ctrl), allowing an external debugger to access protected assets without proper authentication.
obligations.subject: Debug module slave (dm_slave)
obligations.object: Boot ROM, cryptographic accelerators (AES, SHA, HMAC), fuse data, peripheral control registers
obligations.expected_guard: All dm_slave transactions must be subject to the same access-control matrix (acc_ctrl_c) and lock-bit mediation as CPU-originated transactions; debug access should be enabled only after successful JTAG authentication (jtag_unlock) and/or DM active state.
obligations.candidate_path: dm_slave_req/we/addr/wdata → internal bus fabric/decode → target peripheral; debug requests flow from JTAG DTM/DMI (dmi_req, debug_req_bits_op) and may initiate dm_slave operations.
state_conditions_to_check: After reset (debug authentication incomplete)
Before jtag_unlock is asserted
After jtag_unlock is asserted
When peripheral lock bits are set via reglk_ctrl
When priv_lvl_i indicates unprivileged mode
required_evidence_slots: dm_slave address decode and routing in riscv_peripherals.sv
Any conditional gates on dm_slave_we or dm_slave_req using lock/auth signals
Integration of dm_slave into the bus fabric with access-control wrappers (acct/reglk)
obligations.uncertainty: The exact routing of dm_slave requests to peripherals and whether they traverse access-control checks is unclear from this fact layer.

obligations.reason: The JTAG unlock signal (jtag_unlock) is used to override peripheral locks (reglk_wrapper), but it must be generated only after a successful cryptographic challenge (jtag_hash). If the check is bypassed or the unlock can be set by other means (e.g., debug mode, test mode, or unprivileged writes), it would defeat lock protection.
obligations.subject: JTAG debug bridge / unlock logic
obligations.expected_guard: jtag_unlock must be asserted solely after the JTAG password hash (jtag_hash) matches an expected value, and must be cleared on reset or when authentication expires. No other path (debug_mode_i, testmode_i, bus master writes) should be able to influence jtag_unlock.
obligations.candidate_path: JTAG → dmi_jtag → internal unlock logic → jtag_unlock → reglk_wrapper (reglk_ctrl_i) → reglk_ctrl_o.
state_conditions_to_check: After power-on reset
During active debug/testmode_i
When a bus master writes to registers that might alias the unlock control
required_evidence_slots: Generation logic of jtag_unlock in riscv_peripherals.sv
Connection to reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock port
Influence (if any) of testmode_i or debug_mode_i on unlock
obligations.uncertainty: The fact layer shows jtag_hash and jtag_unlock signals but not the comparison logic; verify that the unlock depends solely on a secure hash.

obligations.reason: The fuse read interface (fuse_req, fuse_addr, fuse_rdata) provides a path to potentially sensitive key material or security configuration. Without access control, any bus master (CPU, DMA, debug) could read fuse values, defeating hardware-enforced confidentiality.
obligations.subject: Any bus master
obligations.object: Fuse data (key, lifecycle state, debug authorization)
obligations.expected_guard: Fuse reads must be gated such that only trusted initiators (e.g., a privileged CPU core during boot, or a secure hardware block) can access them; the read path must be blocked or filtered based on requester ID, privilege level, or a dedicated lock bit that is set early in boot.
obligations.candidate_path: AXI/NoC request → address decode → fuse_req/addr → fuse_rdata → response back; fuse interface signals are directly visible in riscv_peripherals.sv.
state_conditions_to_check: During normal operation after boot
When a lock bit (reglk) for the fuse region is set
When the request comes from a non-privileged master or DMA
required_evidence_slots: Decode logic that generates fuse_req from bus transactions
Any lock gate or privilege check on fuse_req/fuse_addr
obligations.uncertainty: The specific decode and protection mechanism for fuses is not provided; need to confirm whether a permission guard exists.

obligations.reason: The reset wrapper (rst_wrapper) produces per‑peripheral resets (rst_1…rst_13). If debug mode (debug_mode_i) or an unauthenticated bus write can trigger a reset to a security-critical peripheral (e.g., AES, boot ROM, lock logic), it may clear locks, erase key material in an uncontrolled way, or leave the system in an insecure state.
obligations.subject: Debug mode / bus master
obligations.object: Peripheral reset lines (rst_1…rst_13), including those for crypto engines or lock state.
obligations.expected_guard: Reset assertion to locked/security-sensitive peripherals should be disallowed while the peripheral is locked or should require a higher privilege level than normal write; debug_mode_i alone should not be sufficient to override lock protections without explicit authentication.
obligations.candidate_path: rst_wrapper takes debug_mode_i, acct_ctrl_i, reglk_ctrl_i and controls rst_n outputs; any accessible register that controls rst_wrapper’s state could initiate a reset.
state_conditions_to_check: When a peripheral lock bit is set
When debug_mode_i is active but JTAG is not authenticated
When an unprivileged bus write targets the reset control register
required_evidence_slots: Logic inside rst_wrapper that generates individual resets (rst_1…rst_13)
Dependencies on debug_mode_i, reglk_ctrl_i, and bus writes
Any protection that prevents reset of locked peripherals
obligations.uncertainty: The precise reset conditions and the effect of debug_mode_i inside rst_wrapper need to be examined in the actual RTL.

obligations.reason: The DMA interface (buf_ariane_dma_*) allows high‑throughput data movement. If DMA reads or writes to protected peripherals (e.g., AES key registers, lock control registers, fuse area) are not subject to the same access‑control checks as CPU accesses, an attacker could use DMA to copy secrets or disable locks.
obligations.subject: DMA engine
obligations.object: All on‑chip mapped peripherals, especially those with lock or key storage
obligations.expected_guard: DMA transactions must be tagged with a privilege level or a security attribute that is checked against acc_ctrl_c and reglk_ctrl before granting access to locked/peripheral regions; DMA should not be able to bypass the access‑control matrix.
obligations.candidate_path: DMA NoC master interface → peripheral interconnect → target peripheral.
state_conditions_to_check: During a DMA transfer while the destination peripheral is locked
When the DMA descriptor is configured by an unprivileged CPU
required_evidence_slots: Connection from DMA bus to the address decode and access‑control logic
Any privilege/lock check on DMA read/write channels
obligations.uncertainty: The fact layer shows only the DMA bus port; the actual integration with access‑control wrappers is not shown.

obligations.reason: Write‑enable flags (we_flag_0 … we_flag_4) appear to control write access to different address regions or peripherals. If these flags can be set by an unprivileged master or remain sticky after a reset that does not properly clear them, privileged write‑protection can be bypassed.
obligations.subject: Any bus master (CPU, debug, DMA) that controls we_flag inputs
obligations.operation: write to we_flag registers or signals
obligations.expected_guard: The we_flag signals must only be assertable under strict conditions, e.g., after a privileged write, during a secure boot phase, or after lock release; they must be cleared by system reset or a lock event.
obligations.candidate_path: Inputs riscv_peripherals.we_flag_0…we_flag_4 → internal use in access control (possibly acc_ctrl logic) → enabling writes to protected regions.
state_conditions_to_check: After any reset (cold/warm)
When the corresponding lock bit is set
When the requestor privilege is insufficient
required_evidence_slots: RTL that drives we_flag_i from external logic
Usage of we_flag in write‑enabling peripheral decoders
Reset/default value of we_flag
obligations.uncertainty: The exact role of we_flag and its persistence across resets needs verification in the RTL.

obligations.reason: The CPU privilege level (priv_lvl_i) is used in the ROM access check (rom_req = rom_req_acct && acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][0]). If this same privilege input is not consistently applied to all other peripheral access paths (e.g., CLINT, cryptographic accelerators, debug registers), then a lower‑privilege master may access functions intended only for higher privilege modes.
obligations.subject: Any bus master that does not carry priv_lvl_i
obligations.object: Peripherals that are supposed to be privilege‑restricted (CLINT, PLIC, crypto CSRs, lock registers)
obligations.expected_guard: Every transaction to a protected peripheral address range must be accompanied by a valid privilege level that is checked against an access‑control table (acc_ctrl_c); requests without such a level (e.g., from DMA or debug) must either be treated as highest privilege only if explicitly authorized, or must be blocked.
obligations.candidate_path: Various NoC interfaces (clint, plic, aes, sha, hmac, etc.) → internal decode → privilege check (or lack thereof).
state_conditions_to_check: Transaction initiated by a non‑CPU master (debug, DMA)
When priv_lvl_i indicates user‑mode but the master is not the CPU
required_evidence_slots: Mapping from NoC requester ID to priv_lvl_i
Usage of priv_lvl_i in the decode/access logic of each peripheral
Access‑control array acc_ctrl_c generation and usage
obligations.uncertainty: The fact layer shows that priv_lvl_i is used only for ROM; we need to check whether other peripheral accesses are also gated by privilege.

obligations.reason: Crypto accelerators (AES0/1/2, SHA256, HMAC) output data back to the bus (ariane_aesN_buf_noc3_data_o etc.). If their internal state registers (keys, intermediate ciphertext, plaintext) are readable under the normal read‑response path without an explicit lock or privilege check, secret material may leak to any bus master.
obligations.subject: Any bus master reading from crypto accelerator memory‑mapped registers
obligations.object: Crypto accelerator internal state registers (key, plaintext, hash state)
obligations.expected_guard: Read access to any register that could expose secret data must be conditionally allowed only when the peripheral is unlocked and the reader has sufficient privilege; alternatively, such registers should be readable only if explicitly enabled by a lock‑protected control bit.
obligations.candidate_path: AXI read request → crypto wrapper → internal register mux → ariane_aesN_buf_noc3_data_o → bus response.
state_conditions_to_check: After key material has been loaded
When the crypto unit is in a locked state
During or immediately after an operation
required_evidence_slots: Decode of read‑only register windows inside crypto wrappers
Lock protection on readable data paths
Any conditional gate on data output based on lock or privilege
obligations.uncertainty: The internal implementation of the accelerators is not provided; we must verify whether secret‑sensitive registers are exposed in the bus read path.

evidence_slots.subject: Debug module slave (dm_slave)
evidence_slots.expected_guard: dm_slave transactions must be routed through the same access-control matrix (acc_ctrl_c) and lock mediation as CPU transactions, and only enabled after JTAG authentication.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: The dm_slave is generated from dm_master via axi2mem and connects only to the debug module's internal slave registers. The debug master (dm_master) that could access the system bus is completely tied off (gnt, r_valid, r_rdata all set to '0). The dm_slave does not provide a path to other peripherals; it is entirely contained within the debug module.
evidence_slots.path: dm_master (AXI bus tied off) → axi2mem → dm_slave_req/we/addr/wdata → internal DM slave (not connected to other peripherals)
evidence_slots.state_condition: After reset, before/after jtag_unlock, with lock bits set – dm_master is always disconnected from system bus.
evidence_slots.impact: No debug bypass to protected peripherals exists because the debug master interface is disabled.

rtl_evidence.description: dm_master instantiated as AXI_BUS, then i_dm_axi2mem converts it to dm_slave signals. The dm_slave is used only within the debug module.
System bus accesses from debug master are tied off: dm_master_gnt = '0, dm_master_r_valid = '0, dm_master_r_rdata = '0.
All other dm_master AXI signals tied to default values; no connection to the main interconnect.

inspection_records.candidate_finding: No violation – the debug slave path does not reach any on-chip peripheral.
inspection_records.notes: The debug master interface is explicitly disabled; therefore, even authenticated debug access via JTAG cannot issue transactions to protected peripherals.

evidence_slots.subject: JTAG unlock logic
evidence_slots.expected_guard: jtag_unlock asserted only after successful hash comparison, and no other source can drive it.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: jtag_unlock is generated exclusively by the dmi_jtag module, which receives jtag_hash, ikey_hash, okey_hash, and we_flag_4. No other RTL in riscv_peripherals assigns jtag_unlock. reglk_wrapper uses jtag_unlock as a reset condition for its lock memory.
evidence_slots.path: dmi_jtag (internal logic) → jtag_unlock → reglk_wrapper.jtag_unlock → reglk_mem reset.
evidence_slots.state_condition: Any state; jtag_unlock is entirely under control of dmi_jtag.
evidence_slots.impact: If dmi_jtag implements a robust hash comparison, the unlock is secure; if bypassable, locks can be cleared.

rtl_evidence.description: dmi_jtag instantiation with jtag_hash_i, okey_hash_i, ikey_hash_i, we_flag, and jtag_unlock_o.
reglk_wrapper uses jtag_unlock in reset condition: if(~(rst_ni && ~jtag_unlock && ~rst_9)) reglk_mem <= 0.

inspection_records.missing_or_uncertain_evidence: No visibility into dmi_jtag internals; cannot confirm the hash comparison logic or whether testmode_i/debug_mode_i influence jtag_unlock.
inspection_records.candidate_finding: Inconclusive – the architecture appears correct, but the internal dmi_jtag implementation must be audited.
inspection_records.notes: Proceed with black-box analysis of dmi_jtag module before final verdict.

evidence_slots.subject: Fuse read interface
evidence_slots.expected_guard: Fuse reads must be gated by privilege/lock; only trusted initiators should be allowed.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: fuse_req, fuse_addr, fuse_rdata are generated inside pkt_wrapper, which receives access-control inputs reglk_ctrl_i (from reglk array) and acct_ctrl_i (acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][5]). The wrapper likely enforces some form of access control, but its internal implementation is not visible.
evidence_slots.path: pkt_wrapper → fuse_req/addr → pkt internal logic → fuse_rdata.
evidence_slots.state_condition: When fuse region is locked or privilege is low; access control is provided but not verified.
evidence_slots.impact: If pkt_wrapper fails to block unauthorized reads, secret fuse data could leak.

rtl_evidence.description: i_pkt_wrapper instantiation with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i connections; fuse interface ports.

inspection_records.missing_or_uncertain_evidence: pkt_wrapper source code not provided; cannot verify how fuse read requests are gated.
inspection_records.candidate_finding: Inconclusive – the architecture shows potential for correct guarding, but internal implementation needs review.

evidence_slots.subject: Reset wrapper (rst_wrapper)
evidence_slots.expected_guard: Resets to locked peripherals must be prevented; debug_mode_i alone must not trigger resets without authentication.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: rst_wrapper generates resets via start/rst_id registers that are writable only when both AXI access control (en_acct && acct_ctrl_i) and the corresponding reglk lock bit allow it. debug_mode_i is an unconnected input – it is not used in any reset logic.
evidence_slots.path: AXI write → en_acct && acct_ctrl_i → reglk_ctrl_i[1] → start/rst_id → rst_mem → peripheral resets.
evidence_slots.state_condition: When lock bit reglk_ctrl[1] is set, writes to start/rst_id are ignored; debug_mode_i high has no effect.
evidence_slots.impact: No bypass: resets are protected by the access-control matrix and lock bits; debug mode cannot force a reset.

rtl_evidence.description: Reset logic: start and rst_id are only written if en && we and the lock bit (reglk_ctrl_i[1]) allows; otherwise they keep previous values.
rst_mem assignment uses case on rst_id only when start is high; otherwise rst_mem cleared.
debug_mode_i is an input but is not referenced in any procedural block; it only appears in the module port list.

inspection_records.candidate_finding: Satisfied: reset control is properly guarded by access and lock bits; debug_mode_i is inert.

evidence_slots.subject: DMA engine
evidence_slots.expected_guard: DMA must be subject to the same access-control matrix as CPU, using privilege and lock checks.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: The DMA wrapper instantiation (i_dma_wrapper) receives reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i signals, just like other peripherals. This indicates that DMA transactions are filtered through the same lock and privilege checks.
evidence_slots.path: buf_ariane_dma_noc2_* → noc_axilite_bridge → dma_wrapper (with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i) → AXI response.
evidence_slots.state_condition: When DMA accesses a locked peripheral, the wrapper will deny access if the lock bit is set and privilege is insufficient.
evidence_slots.impact: No bypass: DMA is integrated into the access-control infrastructure.

rtl_evidence.description: i_dma_wrapper instantiation with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_i connections.

inspection_records.missing_or_uncertain_evidence: Internal DMA wrapper logic not reviewed; assume it enforces the provided access-control signals.
inspection_records.candidate_finding: Satisfied at the integration level; deeper review of dma_wrapper recommended.

evidence_slots.subject: Write-enable flags (we_flag_0..we_flag_4)
evidence_slots.expected_guard: we_flag must only be asserted under strict, secure conditions; not by unprivileged masters.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: we_flag_1 is ORed into the reglk_ctrl byte for AES0, effectively overriding its lock bit. In acct_wrapper, we_flag is ORed into the lowest access-control output bit, forcing write-enable for one peripheral slot. The source of we_flag signals is external to riscv_peripherals and not visible in the provided RTL.
evidence_slots.path: External we_flag_1 → OR with reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] → aes0_wrapper; external we_flag → acct_wrapper.acc_ctrl_o[7:0] forced high.
evidence_slots.state_condition: If an attacker can set we_flag_1, the AES0 lock is disabled; if we_flag can be set, acct write-protection is weakened.
evidence_slots.impact: Potential bypass of lock protection, depending on outside control of we_flag pins.

rtl_evidence.description: AES0 reglk_ctrl_i = reglk_ctrl[1*8+7:1*8] | we_flag_1 – we_flag_1 can force the lock byte to enable writes.
acc_ctrl_o[7:0] = acct_mem[0] | {8{we_flag}} – we_flag forces write-enable for peripheral 0.

inspection_records.missing_or_uncertain_evidence: No visibility into the source of we_flag signals; they are external inputs to the chip/macro.
inspection_records.candidate_finding: Candidate violation: we_flag signals can override lock and access-control on AES0 and potentially other peripherals. The security depends entirely on these external signals being driven securely.
inspection_records.notes: Recommend reviewing the chip-level integration to ensure we_flag pins are tied to secure sources (e.g., write-once fuses or secure boot logic).

evidence_slots.subject: Privilege level (priv_lvl_i)
evidence_slots.expected_guard: CLINT and PLIC (which contain privilege-sensitive CSRs) must be protected by priv_lvl_i and acc_ctrl_c.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: CLINT instantiation (i_clint) receives only testmode_i; no acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i. PLIC instantiation similarly lacks access-control inputs. Other peripherals (AES, SHA, HMAC, Reglk, etc.) all receive acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i] inputs. This means CLINT and PLIC are accessible without privilege or lock checks.
evidence_slots.path: AXI request → clint/plic wrapper → no privilege check → internal registers.
evidence_slots.state_condition: Any privilege level can read/write CLINT and PLIC registers, including user-mode code.
evidence_slots.impact: Violation: machine-mode timer/interrupt registers exposed to unprivileged software, allowing denial-of-service or privilege escalation.

rtl_evidence.description: i_clint instantiation has no acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i connections; only testmode_i.
i_plic instantiation has no acct_ctrl_i or reglk_ctrl_i connections.
Example of protected peripheral: aes0_wrapper receives acc_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1].

inspection_records.candidate_finding: Confirmed violation: CLINT and PLIC are not gated by privilege or lock, contradicting the expected guard.
inspection_records.notes: This is a clear design oversight; need to add access-control wrappers around CLINT and PLIC.

evidence_slots.subject: Crypto accelerator internal state
evidence_slots.expected_guard: Read access to sensitive registers must be blocked when peripheral is locked or privilege is insufficient.
evidence_slots.observed_behavior: All crypto wrappers (aes0, aes1, aes2, sha256, hmac) receive acct_ctrl_i and reglk_ctrl_i. The AXI-lite interface inside the wrapper gates transactions with en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i, thereby blocking any reads or writes when the access-control bit is not set. This denies bus access to the entire peripheral address space, preventing register reads.
evidence_slots.path: AXI read → wrapper acct_ctrl_i gate → internal registers; if acct_ctrl_i low, no transaction passes.
evidence_slots.state_condition: When peripheral lock is set or privilege insufficient, acc_ctrl_c is low, blocking reads.
evidence_slots.impact: No exposure: secret data cannot be read from the bus when access is restricted.

rtl_evidence.description: aes0_wrapper instantiated with reglk_ctrl_i and acct_ctrl_c[priv_lvl_i][1].
Typical wrapper uses en = en_acct && acct_ctrl_i to gate all AXI transactions.

inspection_records.candidate_finding: Satisfied: crypto accelerators are protected by the same access-control framework, preventing unauthorized reads.
inspection_records.notes: Internal wrapper implementations not reviewed, but the interface enforces strict access gating; no visible bypass.
